sentences.text: From Westerbork, we were sent to Theresienstadt, the four of us.
My parents, my sister, and I together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrive in Theresienstadt, and we, again, were assigned different jobs.
My sister, I remember, worked at the farm.
They had farms outside the camp, and I think my mother was at some kind of an industry called [?
Glimmer ?], which they-- the women had to work, and it had something to do with the defense industry.
I really don't know exactly what it was.
I was assigned, again, to the carpenter shop, which wasn't too hard of work.
The unique thing in-- the unique thing in Theresienstadt was we got paid.
I got paid for my work with money.
We had are own money.
You probably have seen it.
It's printed, and it says Theresienstadt and who the [GERMAN] was and so forth.
The only problem with the money was you were unable to buy anything with it.
They did have a store with items showing in a window.

And then I learned that this was all done because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe Theresienstadt was helped by the Swiss Red Cross and-- to put on a show to make believe that this is the way it really was, which it wasn't.
Whenever we got the inspection from the Swiss Cross, when the people came, then everything was put to show and make believe that, look how good we treat the Jews.

And I remember the little children had to run up to the commander when he walked with his inspectors from Switzerland through camp and make believe that, you know, do you have any candy for us today and so forth?
That was Theresienstadt.
I didn't have any bad experience there.
Of course, food was always a problem.
You never had enough to eat.
And the food, you know, you were getting used to it already.
It was always either potatoes and beets or carrots or stuff like that.
But I was not exposed to any mistreatments or violence or anything like that.
But we did have to work hard.
We had to work in barracks, built barracks, and make furniture for the guard houses and so forth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until one day, of course, they started deporting people from Theresienstadt to further east.
We didn't know where, but to-- to perform labor into the country.
And sure enough, one day my dad and I were called, and we were supposed to be sent to other factories.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say that's when my first bad experience started.
We were pushed into a cattle car.
I don't know how many at one time.
When it was?
No.
It must have been in '44, beginning '44 when it was done.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how many we were, but that was a very bad, terrible situation.
I'll never forget that.
And when those doors were closed and nobody had enough room to sit or stand, and people became violent.
And then it was-- it's hard to describe.
And of course, we didn't have any sanitary conditions there.
We didn't have any food.
And how long we were in that car, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] time element is a problem when you get into a concentration camp.
It's hard to explain.
But we didn't have any watches.
We didn't have any calenders.
We didn't know what day it is, what year it is, what time it is.
You just existed.
You lived from one day to the next, and you hoped that tomorrow the war is over and it's-- it's back to normal again.
So how long we were in this situation?
I really don't know.
But I do remember, the only thing I-- I was hoping for to get out of that situation as fast as possible.
It was really unbearable.
People were fighting with each other.
It was sad.
Were you near your father?
Yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sure enough then the train, once it stopped.
And then, it went back and then we moved again, and then we were standing still for God knows how long.
And then finally, when the doors opened, we were in Auschwitz.
I didn't know at that time what Auschwitz was or that we were in Auschwitz.
But then the doors opened, the SS stood there with their machine guns and with their dogs and they were screaming [SPEAKING GERMAN].
We had to leave everything, little things that we still had, behind.
And I remember I had a coat, and-- which I had from home.
And I remember when we prepared for deportation, my mother had sewn in some extra money into the lining just in case that you need some extra money.
So I was-- I wanted to take my coat with me, but I wasn't allowed to.
I had to leave everything there.
We just had to get out of the train, line up in fives, and then we were marched through the gate where you probably have seen them in pictures.
Arbeit macht frei-- labor sets you free.
And I was still under the impression, here it's labor.
We have to work, so big deal.
So we work.
And of course, once we got through the gate, and we were in the camp, the first thing we encountered was the selection.
And this SS officer stood there with this little cane, and he decided that who goes to this side and who goes to that side.
And that's when-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's when my father went to one side, and I went to the other side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that-- that was the last time I ever saw my dad.
I never saw him again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then, of course, the selection consisted of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] twins.
If there were any twins, they were separated.
If you had any tattoos on your body, you were selected.
We discovered after the war that these people were put to death and the tattoo skins were used for lamp shades for a overseer, a woman by the name of Ilse Koch.
And Ilse Koch was tried.
Those lamp shades were used as evidence against her.
After that, we were-- we had to undress, naked, and they deloused us, which meant they had other prisoners.
And they used some kinds of disinfectant on your body, and you were shaved.
All your hair on your body and your head.
And the Germans were always afraid of diseases.
They were very particular.
They always made sure that you don't carry any like lice or were carriers of diseases.
And then, of course, we got our number tattooed, our prison number, and we got, I remember, a striped jacket and striped pants and a cap, and we got two shoes.
And they weren't necessarily a left and a right one.
And I remember distinctly those people who had two left shoes or two right shoes, they had to find somebody else who had the opposite.
And luckily, you had two shoes.

It turned out that later, this was a very important thing in life at camp, because if you had shoes that didn't fit or caused any infection and you were unable to walk straight, that was a reason-- you know, we didn't have any hospitals or-- or-- or medications-- that sometimes could have led to problems, and you-- and we had other selections in camp that you were not found to be qualified to go on.
It's number B, the letter B11668.
You never forget those.
After that, we were marched to a camp that's adjacent to Auschwitz.
It's called Birkenau.
And we were in Birkenau.
And there I met a prisoner who had been there before, who was sent there before.
He had been there for some time.
There was a communication problem, because there were people from all countries.
Most of them where from the eastern countries.
And of course, we had problems-- problems to communicate.
And this man who was Dutch, he said to me, he said, do you people really know where you are here?
We're here.
You know, we're gonna work.
They'll probably assign us to factories.

And I really wasn't under the impression at that time that, since my father was sent to the other side, who was at the time maybe in his early 50's that he probably would get an assignment of a different kind of work.
And I, who was only, you know, in my teens, probably would be sent to the more physical work.
And so I wasn't really so much concerned.
Eventually, I'd probably see him again.
You know.
But this man said to me, he said, you know, this is really the end of the line.
This is where people get gassed and cremated.
And he said, you know the gate where you walk in, he said, and this is where you'll walk out.
And he pointed to these chimneys, which I hadn't observed before.
And they were smoking, which I didn't pay any attention to it.
And then, he explained to us the process what happened to those people who went to the other side.
And I-- I just didn't want to believe it.
You know.
But unfortunately, then we learned the truth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day in Birkenau, I mean, the food there, this-- this camp was really-- you can't describe what you saw there.
This camp where we were in Birkenau, it's called a zigeunerlager, a gypsy camp, which was named after the gypsies who were brought there.
And they all were just gassed.
There was no-- they didn't even select them.
They were just-- the whole camp was emptied out one night, and they all were gassed.
So we were there probably waiting.
You know, we probably be next.
Food was, I mean, there the food was really low.
We didn't get anything to eat so to speak of.
In the morning, we used to get a little piece of bread.
And in the afternoon you had little plate.
We used to get some soup.
It was supposed to be soup.
The bread you felt, well, you take a few bites, and you save a little bit for later.
And-- and pretty soon you learn this doesn't work in-- in Auschwitz.
If one of the other prisoners knows-- knows that you carry a piece of bread on you, you're not gonna have it too long in your possession.
So the end result was, when you get this little piece of bread, you-- you swallow it down as soon as you can.
So you go hungry the rest of the day.
Then, you stand there with your little plate to get the soup, and you have to line up.
If you want to stand in front of the line, you take the chance to get the top of the barrel, which is nothing but just the water.
If you go to the end of the line, many times by the time they get to you, there isn't any left, and you end up with nothing.
So you learn how to fight for your life.
And you try to squeeze in the middle and you hopefully you get some potato peels and some grass or whatever is in there.
And of course, it never was enough.
It never was sufficient.
And I would say what was the worst experience in the concentration camp, it's very simple.
It's hunger.
You're constantly hungry from the time you were there until we got out.
It's-- it's-- there was nothing you wouldn't do to get your hand on a piece of bread.
And even to this day, it's something that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's hard to explain.
How can you explain to somebody what hunger is like?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day in Birkenau, this SS Oberscharfuhrer-- Otto Moll was his name-- he was a big heavy SS man.
And he came, and he announced that if any of the prisoners in my barrack were-- had a trade, they should step forward.
And at this point, there were five of us who always were still together-- boys, you know, like, from Holland.
And we decided we'd try to stay together as long as we can and to help each other.
And of course, I had a trade and somebody else had a trade.
He was a tool and die maker.
And we decided the other three should come along.
And at first, they weren't too sure.
I said, look, what have you got to lose?
If you stay here, you know what chances you take.
It's just a matter of time.
So you have to get out of here.
And we convinced them, and the five of us went.
And this-- there was a truck standing and we had to get on the truck.
And before we went on the truck, this Otto Moll said, he said, all of a sudden Jews are all tradesmen.
Before they were  all businesspeople.
Now, they all have a trade.
He said, but if I discover that one of you lied, he said, you're not going to  live too much longer.
And then, some of our friends got scared that we held on to them, and we went.
And we were transferred to a camp called Gleiwitz.
G-L-E-I-W-I-T-Z.
It used to be a Polish town called Gliwice.
And when the Germans occupied Poland, they made it part of Germany, and they called it Gleiwitz.
It's the same as Auschwitz used to be Oswiecim.
And, you know.
Gleiwitz, I would say, was maybe a half hour ride on the truck.
And what the Germans had done was, this-- this area of Upper Silesia in Germany was a very highly industrial area.
They had a lot of factories there.
And at that time, of course, most of them worked for the war-- ammunition factories and so forth.
The Buna was IG Farben.
It's like a big chemical factory.
Krupp was the metal factory.
They were all in that area.
And what the Germans had done was they had set up little labor camps holding about 2,000 prisoners to subsidize the labor force in those factories.
So there were civilian workers in those factories.
Let's say, so many and then they injected so many prisoners to subsidize to help with the work that the other civilians were doing.
And so we got to this camp called Gleiwitz I.
There were three camps in Gleiwitz, and Gleiwitz I was assigned.
We had to work in a-- what they call [GERMAN].
The-- [GERMAN] means railroad.
The railroad in Germany was federally owned, so this was a railroad repair shop for their system.
All the railroad cars that were damaged from air attacks or other problems were sent to this factory.
And we had to reinstall them again.
We had to repair them again for work.
And my assignment was with a group of-- there were two Polish women in my assignment of the [GERMAN], what they call it.
Our responsibility was when a car had arrived at the factory, we were supposed to bring in that car and transfer to the appropriate area what that particular work had to be done.
So which it had really nothing to do with, thank God, with carpentry work.
And therefore, we were in good shape.
We were able to do that kind of work.
I was very fortunate because the other two women, the civilian women that worked with the two of us, were kind of very nice.
We couldn't communicate because I didn't speak Polish, and I didn't speak German or Dutch.
But I remember every period of time, we had to change shifts from day shift to night shift.
And the factory supplied during the work shift a period where they could, you know, take a break, where they had their lunch.
And they-- they had a kitchen there that prepared one particular food like mixed potatoes with vegetables and sometimes a piece of ham or so on there.
And that was free for the workers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Luckily, the women that are with me didn't like that food.
They always brought their own sandwiches along.
But what they did when they worked night shift-- and this was dangerous-- when they went to get their food, they'd make believe they ate it.
And then they hid it somewhere.
They made sure that we saw where they hid it.
And then when the opportunity came, we were able to go there and gobble it down.
Now, this was only possible during the night shift.
During the weeks that we had the day shift, it was too dangerous because there were too many more people there watching.
And this was a-- a problem.
Looking back, I think if I wouldn't have had this extra food that we got when we had the night shift, possibly I would have been reduced to the stage of what we called a Muselmann much earlier.
And then we did have selections in Gleiwitz every period of time to make sure that we were still able to produce.
And sometimes we had to jump to make sure that we were able to jump far enough.
And if the SS person in charge thought that you didn't look right or you weren't physically able or capable, then there was a truck standing there.
And you had to go up on the truck.
When the truck was full, then the truck went back to Auschwitz.
And the truck came back with the same amount of people, let's say, who were new arrivals.
And this was a constant exchange.
That situation was always very sad, because you knew and they knew once you get on that truck what the end was gonna be.
And you never would see them.
And some of the ones that had to get up on the truck, you knew for so long and went through for such long periods.
And you always felt bad.
And those were very sad moments, I would say.
Well, we don't know for sure.
But the general [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] idea was they went straight to the gas chamber, because I don't know anybody that I knew who went on that truck.
I don't know any of those that survived.
I never saw any of them ever again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Work was very hard.
We [? do ?] [? had ?] to work.
This was hard physical work.
And of course, food never was enough.
After we went back to work, we could volunteer to do additional kitchen work.
We were allowed to peel potatoes or peel vegetables for the guards for their food.
Of course, you tried to volunteer because that gave you an opportunity that you were able to steal a few things and eat it while you were doing it.
When you left the kitchen, you were checked to make sure that you didn't take any food with you.
But at least we had an opportunity to swallow a couple of raw potatoes or carrots, whatever was there.
Otto Moll was an individual who was a sadist.
He was an alcoholic, and most of the time he was drunk.
And that's when I really came eye to eye with executions and violent behavior.
And you see how people were killed and hanged.
And a friend of mine got 30 lashes, because he was caught praying one morning.
And we had to carry him back.
This was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you can't describe it with words what we saw there.
